feat.name: Mark of Warding
entries: You gain the following benefits.

entries.name: Warder's Intuition
entries: When you make an Intelligence ({@skill Investigation|XPHB}) check or an ability check using {@item Thieves' Tools|XPHB}, you can roll {@dice 1d4} and add the number rolled to the ability check.

entries.name: Wards and Seals
entries: You always have the {@spell Alarm|XPHB} and {@spell Kundarak Seal|BakerKanon} spells prepared. You can cast each spell once without a spell slot, and you regain the ability to cast it in that way when you finish a {@variantrule Long Rest|XPHB}.

You can also cast these spells using any spell slots you have of the appropriate level. Intelligence, Wisdom, or Charisma is your spellcasting ability for these spells (choose when you select this feat).
When you reach character level 3, you also always have the {@spell Knock|XPHB} spell prepared and can cast it the same way.

entries.name: Spells of the Mark
entries: If you have the Spellcasting or Pact Magic class feature, the spells on the Mark of Warding Spells table are added to that feature's spell list.

entries.caption: Mark of Warding Spells
rows: {@spell Leomund's Secret Chest|XPHB|Durbannek's Secret Chest}*, {@spell Mordenkainen's Private Sanctum|XPHB|Ghedin's Private Sanctum}*

feat.name: Mark of Scribing
entries: You gain the following benefits.

entries.name: Gifted Scribe
entries: When you make an Intelligence ({@skill History|XPHB}) check or an ability check using {@item Calligrapher's Supplies|XPHB}, {@item Cartographer's Tools|XPHB}, or a {@item Forgery Kit|XPHB}, you can roll {@dice 1d4} and add the number rolled to the ability check.

entries.name: Scribe's Insight
entries: You know the {@spell Sivis Sigil|BakerKanon} cantrip. You also always have the {@spell Comprehend Languages|XPHB} spell prepared. You can cast it once without a spell slot, and you regain the ability to cast it in that way when you finish a {@variantrule Short Rest|XPHB|Short} or {@variantrule Long Rest|XPHB}. When you reach character level 3, you also always have the {@spell Whispering Wind|BakerKanon} spell prepared and you regain the ability to cast it in that way when you finish {@variantrule Long Rest|XPHB}.

You can also cast these spells using any spell slots you have of the appropriate level. Intelligence, Wisdom, or Charisma is your spellcasting ability for these spells (choose when you select this feat).

entries.name: Tool Proficiency
entries: Choose one of the following tools: {@item Calligrapher's Supplies|XPHB}, {@item Cartographer's Tools|XPHB}, or {@item Forgery Kit|XPHB}. If you lack proficiency with the chosen tool, you gain proficiency with it. If you already have proficiency with the chosen tool or gain it from another source in the future, you gain {@variantrule Expertise|XPHB} with it.

entries.name: Spells of the Mark
entries: If you have the Spellcasting or Pact Magic class feature, the spells on the Mark of Scribing Spells table are added to that feature's spell list.

feat.name: Mark of Hospitality
entries: You gain the following benefits.

entries.name: Ever Hospitable
entries: When you make a Charisma ({@skill Persuasion|XPHB}) check or an ability check using {@item Brewer's Supplies|XPHB} or {@item Cook's Utensils|XPHB}, you can roll {@dice 1d4} and add the number rolled to the ability check.

entries.name: Innkeeper's Magic
entries: You always have the {@spell Purify Food and Drink|XPHB} and {@spell Unseen Servant|XPHB} spells prepared. You can cast each spell once without a spell slot, and you regain the ability to cast it in that way when you finish a {@variantrule Long Rest|XPHB}. You can also cast these spells using any spell slots you have of the appropriate level. Intelligence, Wisdom, or Charisma is your spellcasting ability for these spells (choose when you select this feat).
When you reach character level 3, you also always have the {@spell Calm Emotions|XPHB} spell prepared and can cast it the same way

entries.name: Spells of the Mark
entries: If you have the Spellcasting or Pact Magic class feature, the spells on the Mark of Hospitality Spells table are added to that feature's spell list.

entries.caption: Mark of Hospitality Spells
rows: {@spell Prayer of Healing|XPHB} ({@spell Fortifying Feast|BakerKanon}*), {@spell Rope Trick|XPHB}*
{@spell Create Food and Water|XPHB}*, {@spell Leomund's Tiny Hut|XPHB}
{@spell Aura of Purity|XPHB}, {@spell Mordenkainen's Faithful Hound|XPHB}*

feat.name: Mark of Making
entries: You gain the following benefits.

entries.name: Artisan's Intuition
entries: When you make an Intelligence ({@skill Arcana|XPHB}) check or an ability check using {@item Artisan's Tools|XPHB}, you can roll {@dice 1d4} and add the number rolled to the ability check.

entries.name: Spellsmith
entries: You know the {@spell Mending|XPHB} cantrip. You also always have the {@spell Magic Weapon|XPHB} spell prepared. You can cast it once without a spell slot, and you regain the ability to cast it in that way when you finish a {@variantrule Long Rest|XPHB}. You can also cast it using any spell slots you have. Intelligence, Wisdom, or Charisma is your spellcasting ability for these spells (choose when you select this feat).

entries.name: Spells of the Mark
entries: If you have the Spellcasting or Pact Magic class feature, the spells on the Mark of Making Spells table are added to that feature's spell list.

feat.name: Mark of Shadow
entries: You gain the following benefits.

entries.name: Cunning Intuition
entries: When you make a Dexterity ({@skill Stealth|XPHB}) or Charisma ({@skill Performance|XPHB}) check, you can roll {@dice 1d4} and add the number rolled to the ability check.

entries.name: Shape Shadows
entries: You know the {@spell Minor Illusion|XPHB} cantrip. You also always have the {@spell Invisibility|XPHB} spell prepared. You can cast it once without a spell slot, and you regain the ability to cast it in that way when you finish a {@variantrule Long Rest|XPHB}. You can also cast it using any spell slots you have of the appropriate level. Intelligence, Wisdom, or Charisma is your spellcasting ability for these spells (choose when you select this feat).

entries.name: Spells of the Mark
entries: If you have the Spellcasting or Pact Magic class feature, the spells on the Mark of Shadow Spells table are added to that feature's spell list.

feat.name: Mark of Sentinel
entries: You gain the following benefits.

entries.name: Sentinel's Intuition
entries: When you make a Wisdom ({@skill Insight|XPHB} or {@skill Perception|XPHB}) check, you can roll {@dice 1d4} and add the number rolled to the ability check.

entries.name: Guardian's Shield
entries: You always have the {@spell Shield|XPHB} spell prepared. You can cast it once without a spell slot, and you regain the ability to cast it in that way when you finish a {@variantrule Long Rest|XPHB}. You can also cast it using any spell slots you have. Intelligence, Wisdom, or Charisma is your spellcasting ability for this spell (choose when you select this feat).

entries.name: Vigilant Guardian
entries: When a creature you can see within 5 feet of you is hit by an attack roll, you can take a {@variantrule Reaction|XPHB} to swap places with that creature, and you are hit by the attack instead. You can use this feature a number of times equal to your {@variantrule Proficiency|XPHB|Proficiency Bonus} and regain all expended uses when you finish a {@variantrule Long Rest|XPHB}.

entries.name: Spells of the Mark
entries: If you have the Spellcasting or Pact Magic class feature, the spells on the Mark of Sentinel Spells table are added to that feature's spell list.

feat.name: Mark of Handling
entries: You gain the following benefits.

entries.name: Wild Intuition
entries: When you make an Intelligence ({@skill Nature|XPHB}) or Wisdom ({@skill Animal Handling|XPHB}) check, you can roll {@dice 1d4} and add the number rolled to the ability check.

entries.name: Primal Connection
entries: You always have the {@spell Animal Friendship|XPHB} and {@spell Speak with Animals|XPHB} spells prepared. You can cast each spell once without a spell slot, and you regain the ability to cast it in that way when you finish a {@variantrule Long Rest|XPHB}. You can also cast these spells using any spell slots you have. Intelligence, Wisdom, or Charisma is your spellcasting ability for these spells (choose when you select this feat).
When you reach character level 3, you can target a Beast when you cast {@spell Calm Emotions|XPHB}.

entries.name: Monstrous Connections
entries: When you reach character level 3, you can target a Monstrosity when you cast {@spell Animal Friendship|XPHB} or {@spell Speak with Animals|XPHB} if the creature's Intelligence score is 3 or lower.

entries.name: Spells of the Mark
entries: If you have the Spellcasting or Pact Magic class feature, the spells on the Mark of Handling Spells table are added to that feature's spell list.

feat.name: Mark of Passage
entries: You gain the following benefits.

entries.name: Courier's Speed
entries: Your {@variantrule Speed|XPHB} increases by 5 feet.

entries.name: Intuitive Motion
entries: When you make a Strength ({@skill Athletics|XPHB}) or Dexterity ({@skill Acrobatics|XPHB}) check, you can roll {@dice 1d4} and add the number rolled to the ability check.

entries.name: Magical Passage
entries: You always have the {@spell Misty Step|XPHB} spell prepared. You can cast it once without a spell slot, and you regain the ability to cast it in that way when you finish a {@variantrule Long Rest|XPHB}. You can also cast it using any spell slots you have. Intelligence, Wisdom, or Charisma is your spellcasting ability for this spell (choose when you select this feat).

entries.name: Spells of the Mark
entries: If you have the Spellcasting or Pact Magic class feature, the spells on the Mark of Passage Spells table are added to that feature's spell list.

feat.name: Greater Mark of Handling
entries: You gain the following benefits.

entries.name: Improved Intuition
entries: When you use the Wild Intuition benefit of your {@feat Mark of Handling|BakerKanon} feat, you can roll {@dice 1d6} instead of {@dice 1d4}.

entries.name: Improved Handling
entries: While you are mounted, immediately after you hit a target within 5 feet of your mount with a melee attack roll, your mount can take a {@variantrule Reaction|XPHB} to move up to its {@variantrule Speed|XPHB} or take the {@action Attack|XPHB} action to make one attack only (your choice).

entries.name: Subdue Animal
entries: As a {@action Magic|XPHB} action, you exert command over one Beast or Monstrosity you can see within 30 feet of yourself. The target must succeed on a Wisdom saving throw (DC 8 plus your Wisdom modifier and {@variantrule Proficiency|XPHB|Proficiency Bonus}) or have the {@condition Frightened|XPHB} condition until the start of your next turn. You can use this benefit a number of times equal to your {@variantrule Proficiency|XPHB|Proficiency Bonus}, and you regain all expended uses when you finish a {@variantrule Long Rest|XPHB}.

feat.name: Greater Mark of Hospitality
entries: You gain the following benefits.

entries.name: Improved Intuition
entries: When you use the Ever Hospitable benefit of your {@feat Mark of Hospitality|BakerKanon} feat, you can roll {@dice 1d6} instead of {@dice 1d4}.

entries.name: Improved Hospitality
entries: When you cast {@spell Purify Food and Drink|XPHB}, you can modify the spell so that instead of its normal effect, each creature of your choice within 30 feet of you is refreshed. Each affected creature's {@condition Exhaustion|XPHB} level is reduced by 1, and the creature gains {@variantrule Temporary Hit Points|XPHB} equal to your {@variantrule Proficiency|XPHB|Proficiency Bonus} plus your Intelligence, Wisdom, or Charisma modifier (choose when you select this feat). Once you modify the spell with this benefit, you can't do so again until you finish a {@variantrule Long Rest|XPHB}.

feat.name: Greater Mark of Making
entries: You gain the following benefits.

entries.name: Improved Intuition
entries: When you use the Artisan's Intuition benefit of your {@feat Mark of Making|BakerKanon} feat, you can roll {@dice 1d6} instead of {@dice 1d4}.

entries.name: Improved Making
entries: When you use the Spellsmith benefit of your {@feat Mark of Making|BakerKanon} feat to cast {@spell Magic Weapon|XPHB} without a spell slot, you cast the spell as its level 3 version.

feat.name: Greater Mark of Passage
entries: You gain the following benefits.

entries.name: Improved Intuition
entries: When you use the Intuitive Motion benefit of your {@feat Mark of Passage|BakerKanon} feat, you can roll {@dice 1d6} instead of {@dice 1d4}.

entries.name: Improved Passage
entries: When you use the Magical Passage benefit of your {@feat Mark of Passage|BakerKanon} feat to cast {@spell Misty Step|XPHB} without a spell slot, you can also choose up to two willing creatures you can see within 30 feet of yourself before you teleport. Each target can then take a {@variantrule Reaction|XPHB} to also teleport up to 30 feet to an unoccupied space it can see.

feat.name: Greater Mark of Scribing
entries: You gain the following benefits.

entries.name: Improved Intuition
entries: When you use the Gifted Scribe benefit of your {@feat Mark of Scribing|BakerKanon} feat, you can roll {@dice 1d6} instead of {@dice 1d4}.

entries.name: Inspired Scribing
entries: When you cast {@spell Comprehend Languages|XPHB}, you can modify the spell to encompass up to three willing creatures you can see within 30 feet of yourself. Each chosen creature also gains the benefits of the spell for the duration. In addition, for the duration of the spell, you and the chosen creatures can communicate telepathically with each other while within 1 mile of each other. Once you modify the spell with this benefit, you can't do so again until you finish a {@variantrule Long Rest|XPHB}.

feat.name: Greater Mark of Sentinel
entries: You gain the following benefits.

entries.name: Improved Intuition
entries: When you use the Sentinel's Intuition benefit of your {@feat Mark of Sentinel|BakerKanon} feat, you can roll {@dice 1d6} instead of {@dice 1d4}.

entries.name: Improved Sentinel
entries: When you use the Vigilant Guardian benefit of your {@feat Mark of Sentinel|BakerKanon} feat, you can also make one attack with a weapon or an {@variantrule Unarmed Strike|XPHB} as part of that same {@variantrule Reaction|XPHB}.

feat.name: Greater Mark of Shadow
entries: You gain the following benefits.

entries.name: Improved Intuition
entries: When you use the Cunning Intuition benefit of your {@feat Mark of Shadow|BakerKanon} feat, you can roll {@dice 1d6} instead of {@dice 1d4}.

entries.name: Improved Shadow
entries: When you use the Shape Shadows benefit of your {@feat Mark of Shadow|BakerKanon} feat to cast {@spell Invisibility|XPHB} without a spell slot, you cast the spell as its level 3 version.

feat.name: Greater Mark of Warding
entries: You gain the following benefits.

entries.name: Improved Intuition
entries: When you use the Warder's Intuition benefit of your {@feat Mark of Warding|BakerKanon} feat, you can roll {@dice 1d6} instead of {@dice 1d4}.

entries.name: Improved Warding
entries: When a creature makes an attack roll against you or a creature you can see within 30 feet of yourself, you can take a {@variantrule Reaction|XPHB} to impose {@variantrule Disadvantage|XPHB} on that roll. You can use this benefit a number of times equal to your {@variantrule Proficiency|XPHB|Proficiency Bonus}, and you regain all expended uses when you finish a {@variantrule Long Rest|XPHB}.

featFluff.name: Mark of Warding
entries: When an heir of House Kundarak first manifests the Mark of Warding, they are placed in isolation while they master its first two gifts. The first of these is a peaceful source of comfort: the ability to set an {@spell Alarm|XPHB}. As a {@variantrule Ritual|XPHB} spell, this can be cast at any time. The heir studies the space or the portal they wish to ward, and imagines energy flowing from their dragonmark to fill that space. While there is no actual sentience to an {@spell Alarm|XPHB} spell, most Kundarak heirs imagine a guardian—crafting the story of the spirit that serves and protects them. The heir traces the patterns of the Mark of Warding in the air and murmurs instructions to their guardian (the somatic and verbal components of the spell), thanking the spirit for its service and telling it whether to respond to intrusion by warning the heir directly or making a noise. For most Kundarak heirs, this is a comfortable experience; it makes them feel protected and secure. Heirs with a newly manifested Mark are encouraged to cast {@spell Alarm|XPHB} over and over, warding everything in sight. This power poses no danger to anyone. But the second gift of the Mark of Warding is another matter entirely, and this is the reason new heirs must be isolated. Every heir can feel the power of the Mark promising protection. If they let instinct guide them and trace the pattern of the Mark, power flows through the Mark and into that seal, burning with the determination to protect and to punish. This is the {@spell Kundarak Seal|BakerKanon}, the first step toward a {@spell Glyph of Warding|XPHB}. It can be placed on anything that can be opened or closed—a door, a window, a chest—or on the heir's own skin. With time and training, an heir can weave safeguards into the seal, setting a word of safe passage or choosing when to trigger the seal on their skin. But in those first days these traps pose a danger to everyone; a playful shove is enough to trigger a Sigil of Vengeance and injure or kill a friend. Foundlings with the Mark of Warding are rare, and often have tragic tales of loved ones harmed by an Elemental Trap before the heir mastered their powers. This isn't  a danger for long. Once an heir understands this gift, they can contain a Sigil of Vengeance until they choose to unleash its power; most heirs start each morning by tracing the mark on their skin and setting that ward against enemies. This is something everyone knows about Kundarak heirs—You strike a blow against them, and they'll blast you with vengeance. It's something that gives Kundarak bankers and negotiators confidence when dealing with strangers: you harm me at your peril.

Further gifts of the Mark of Warding offer both sides of a coin, as heirs learn both how to protect the things they love and how to bypass protections. An experienced heir can seal anything that can be opened, weaving an {@spell Arcane Lock|XPHB}; but they also possess the power to {@spell Knock|XPHB}, opening any door. They have an intuitive knack for using {@item Thieves' Tools|XPHB} to create traps and locks as well as to remove them; they can set deadly wards, but they also have a talent for {@skill Investigation|XPHB} and for mystically finding traps. Warding-marked heirs often have a fondness for puzzles; there is an intuitive satisfaction that comes from opening a look or unraveling a mystery.

Player characters and remarkable NPCs can master both powers of the Least Mark of Warding, but most heirs only learn one of its two paths. Those drawn to the path of Wards gain the ability to cast {@spell Nondetection|XPHB} and {@spell Mordenkainen's Private Sanctum|XPHB|Private Sanctum}. These allow the heir to shield objects, people, or places from divination magic, and also to prevent teleportation into or out of a particular space. {@spell Mordenkainen's Private Sanctum|XPHB|Private Sanctum} can be made permanent, and most long-established prisons have Kundarak wards that prevent teleportation. Those who follow the path of Seals gain access to {@spell Glyph of Warding|XPHB} and {@spell Leomund's Secret Chest|XPHB|Secret Chest}. These heirs are the trapsmiths of the House, weaving Glyphs into security systems for valued clients.

The final gift of the Greater Mark is one that even player characters have to choose between. The rarer of the two options is the ability to cast {@spell Antilife Shell|XPHB}, keeping all living things from approaching the Warding heir. Those with this gift often worked with House Deneith during the last war; a Warding heir could shield a {@object Siege Staff|ExploringEberron24} crew and keep them from being overrun. Heirs with this power are rare and as the war went on, the use of Warforged constructs and Karrnathi undead reduced its value, but there were still times when these wars played a key role in a battle. The more common gift of the Greater Mark is {@spell Kundarak's Ward|BakerKanon}—a power that weaves {@spell Mordenkainen's Private Sanctum|XPHB|Private Sanctum}, {@spell Arcane Lock|XPHB}, and {@spell Glyph of Warding|XPHB} together to protect a stronghold. Many noble estates have {@item Houseward|ExploringEberron24|Housewards} that maintain {@spell Kundarak's Ward|ExploringEberron24|Kundarak's Wards}. With that said, when an heir casts {@spell Kundarak's Ward|ExploringEberron24} they choose which effects to implement. Most nobles don't want to have their hallways filled with fog!

{@spell Leomund's Secret Chest|XPHB|Durbannek's Secret Chest} and {@spell Mordenkainen's Private Sanctum|XPHB|Ghedim's Private Sanctum} are identical to {@spell Leomund's Secret Chest|XPHB} and {@spell Mordenkainen's Private Sanctum|XPHB}, just using the names of the Kundarak families who specialized in these spells in the early history of the House.

entries.name: Kanon vs Canon
entries: Most of the time I prefer to hold to current canon, but I've made a significant number of changes here. The core abilities remain the same; what I've swapped are the spells. My goal here is to work with the original idea of the Mark, the services Kundarak provides, and what feels logical and cohesive to me. Specifically...

items: Forge of the Artificer assigns {@spell Mage Armor|XPHB} to the base mark and {@spell Armor of Agathys|XPHB} as a spell of the Mark. {@spell Mage Armor|XPHB} is fine, but it's useless for adventurers who use medium or heavy armor, so I shifted that to be the Spell of the Mark and replaced {@spell Armor of Agathys|XPHB} with {@spell Kundarak Seal|BakerKanon}. {@spell Kundarak Seal|BakerKanon} draws on Fire Trap (which was part of the 3.5 Mark) but adds the idea that you can use it to trap YOURSELF. This is similar in concept to {@spell Armor of Agathys|XPHB} and {@spell Hellish Rebuke|XPHB}—you hurt someone who hits you—but you have to cast it in advance. It keeps the idea of Elemental Trap as a service Kundarak heirs can provide, but Sigil of Vengeance is likely to be more useful for the typical adventurer.

The typical adventurer has more use for {@spell Knock|XPHB} than {@spell Arcane Lock|XPHB}, and both are useful for the House businesses, so I made {@spell Knock|XPHB} the spell provided by the base feat and {@spell Arcane Lock|XPHB} the Spell of the Mark.

I swapped {@spell Nystul's Magic Aura|XPHB} with {@spell Find Traps|XPHB} because to me, Magic Aura is more about deception than protection, and that's not something that is reflected in the other spells of the mark. Meanwhile, {@spell Find Traps|XPHB} establishes that the Mark of Warding gives its bearers a broad affinity for traps, which is reflected by the Intuition with {@item Thieves' Tools|XPHB} and is one of their main fields of business. Just as the Mark allows you to lock and unlock things, it makes sense to me that it can help you find traps as well as to set them.

I replaced {@spell Magic Circle|XPHB} with {@spell Nondetection|XPHB} because {@spell Nondetection|XPHB} was an original spell of the mark in 3.5 and because it's consistent with {@spell Mordenkainen's Private Sanctum|XPHB|Private Sanctum}—establishing that the Mark can ward against divination—while there's no other Mark effects that feel aligned to {@spell Magic Circle|XPHB} (such as {@spell Hallow|XPHB} or {@spell Protection From Evil and Good|XPHB}).

I swapped {@spell Mordenkainen's Faithful Hound|XPHB|Faithful Hound} with {@spell Mordenkainen's Private Sanctum|XPHB|Private Sanctum} because I previously assigned {@spell Mordenkainen's Faithful Hound|XPHB|Faithful Hound} to Ghallanda. While {@spell Mordenkainen's Faithful Hound|XPHB|Faithful Hound} isn't a bad option for Warding, {@spell Mordenkainen's Private Sanctum|XPHB|Private Sanctum} is consistent with {@spell Nondetection|XPHB} and it fit to me that Ghallanda could set a spectral blink dog to guard your door while Kundarak would ward a wider area.

{@spell Guards and Wards|XPHB|Guards & Wards} was a key feature of the Mark of Warding in its original incarnation. Aside from being a 6th level spell, the standard {@spell Guards and Wards|XPHB|Guards & Wards} goes kind of over the top (There's spiderwebs in every staircase! There's {@spell Confusion} spells at every intersection!), and I wanted to tone that down a little—and since the Mark now includes {@spell Mordenkainen's Private Sanctum|XPHB|Private Sanctum}, it made sense to weave that in. So {@spell Kundarak's Ward|BakerKanon} is a watered down {@spell Guards and Wards|XPHB|Guards & Wards} with added functionality from {@spell Mordenkainen's Private Sanctum|XPHB|Private Sanctum}. Notably, it doesn't have the "Cast every day for a year to make permanent" clause; it's maintained by {@item Houseward|ExploringEberron24|Housewards}, but you can't make it permanent.

entries.name: {@filter Focus Items|items|source=|type=|property=Warding (Kundarak)}
entries: The ability to cast {@spell Glyph of Warding|XPHB} or {@spell Knock|XPHB} once per day is a useful trick, but if you're going to make a living as a locksmith or selling security systems, you need to be able to produce these effects more frequently and to provide services that will last. Someone poor and in a hurry can pay a Kundarak Warden to cast {@spell Glyph of Warding|XPHB} on their door, but once the spell is triggered it will disperse. If you want to create a Glyph that will last—an ongoing security system for a noble or a prison—you need to use focus items.

The two most common Kundarak focus items are Warden's Wares and Sealing Pigments. Warden's Wares are a specialized form of {@item Thieves' Tools|XPHB}; they are treated as {@item Thieves' Tools|XPHB} for purposes of tool proficiency, but Kundarak heirs can use them to construct magical traps as well as to disarm them. Sealing Pigments are infused with powdered siberys dragonshards. When an heir creates a long-term security system they trace its patterns with Sealing Pigments. The Pigments become {@condition invisible} after the work is complete, but the power remains in place. Long term Kundarak traps often have some sort of focal point as well. Rather than being a glyph, a trap that dispels magic could be a metal plate engraved with the image of a beholder that opens its central eye when the trap is triggered. Sealing Pigments are still used in the creation of this trap, but it's not just a pattern of paint.
Traps and security systems are the primary products of House Kundarak.

featFluff.name: Mark of Shadow
entries: Humans often equate the word shadow with darkness. For the Phiarlan, shadow refers to shadows of reality—the insubstantial images of illusion. The simplest power of the Mark of Shadow is the ability to cast {@spell Minor Illusion} at will, weaving images or sounds. This basic gift is reflected in the heir's bonuses to Dexterity ({@skill Stealth}) and Charisma ({@skill Performance}) checks; an heir can instinctively draw on shadows when hiding or use subtle illusion to improve a performance. Beyond these intuitive bonuses, the power of illusion is something Phiarlan heirs use constantly and casually. An heir seeking to draw attention will conjure a star into their raised hand. One telling a joke might add laughter or a sting to the punchline. When giving directions, an heir can manifest a floating map; when telling a story, they can add sound effects, music, or distinctive voices. The second ability all Dragonmarked heirs possess is {@spell Invisibility|XPHB}. Every heir with the Mark of Shadow can become {@condition invisible} for up to an hour each day. This is a powerful defensive tool; a Phiarlan who's threatened can turn {@condition invisible} and retreat. It's a fantastic gift for a stagehand, who can move around on stage to add elements to a performance without being seen. Phiarlans also use {@spell Invisibility|XPHB} when they desire privacy. A Phiarlan at a boring party might use their skills to liven things up—or they might turn {@condition invisible} and read a book in the corner. When someone disappears at a social occasion without bidding farewell, it's known as a "Phiarlan Goodbye."

{@spell Disguise Self|XPHB} is a Spell of the Mark almost all Shadow heirs gain access to. While this can be used for impersonation—it's a workhorse spell for Serpentine agents—heirs use it in many ways. Phiarlan actors can use the spell to assume the shapes of the characters they play, reducing the need for costuming and allowing multiple actors to easily share a role. Other Phiarlans use {@spell Disguise Self|XPHB} not to deceive others, but rather to enhance their own appearance—transforming clothing, adding makeup, styling hair in impossible ways. A Phiarlan might be disheveled and still in their pajamas, but with {@spell Disguise Self|XPHB} they can greet guests perfectly coiffed and elegantly dressed. For Phiarlan musicians, this can allow dramatic costume changes in the middle of a performance. Phiarlan heirs learn a host of simple applications of {@spell Disguise Self|XPHB} that function much like social media filters—minor cosmetic enhancements for particular situations.

Beyond {@spell Disguise Self|XPHB}, there are two paths of Spells of the Mark. Player characters gain access to all of the spells, but most Phiarlan heirs can only use one of two sets of the spells. Those who are most extroverted generally have access to {@spell Silent Image|XPHB}, {@spell Darkness|XPHB}, {@spell Major Image|XPHB}, {@spell Hallucinatory Terrain|XPHB}, and {@spell Seeming|XPHB}. These gifts are incredibly useful for theatrical work; when enhanced by focus items, {@spell Hallucinatory Terrain|XPHB} and {@spell Seeming|XPHB} allow a stage master to weave a set and costumes from shadow. Conversely, introverted heirs usually develop the ability to cast {@spell Pass Without Trace}, {@spell Clairvoyance|XPHB}, {@spell Greater Invisibility|XPHB} and {@spell Mislead|XPHB}. Such heirs may be drawn into the work of the Serpentine Table, but this path is also common for artists who prefer painting, sculpting, or writing to the company of others; the Mark gives them the power to avoid unwanted distractions.

At first glance, {@spell Clairvoyance|XPHB} is an outlier. Most Spells of the Mark allow the heir to project visions from their imagination into the world. {@spell Clairvoyance|XPHB} does the reverse; it allows them to draw images or sounds from the world into their minds. While {@spell Clairvoyance|XPHB} has obvious value to a spy, it's a gift often manifested by Phiarlan artisans. Painters replicating scenes will often use {@spell Clairvoyance|XPHB} to refer to the inspiration from the comfort of their studios. Other artists often have a particular place that serves as a source of comfort or relaxation. A Phiarlan writer may "go to their quiet place" when dealing with writer's block.

One thing to keep in mind when dealing with House Phiarlan is that its heirs wove shadows long before they possessed the Mark of Shadow. The ancient Phiarlans who traveled across Xen'drik were often accomplished Bards who relied on {@spell Invisibility|XPHB} to evade their enemies and who used illusions to enhance their tales. The Mark of Shadows granted innate abilities to Dragonmarked heirs, but the Phiarlans had been working with illusions for tens of thousands of years before it appeared. Phiarlan heirs who don't manifest the Dragonmark can train at the Demesne of Shadow to learn traditional techniques for summoning illusions; because of this, there's less of an inherent gap between marked and unmarked heirs than you see in some Houses.

entries.name: Phiarlan Verbal and Somatic Components
entries: Whether they are casting Spells of the Mark or bardic magic, heirs of House Phiarlan usually make spellcasting a performance. Verbal components require the caster to speak in at least a normal speaking voice, and somatic components require the caster to have a free hand. But these components don't have to be limited to chanting or gestures. A Phiarlan heir can sing their spells, weaving words of power into their songs. Their gestures can be part of a full dance, or they can just be graceful motions performed with hypnotic rhythm. When the legendary Elorrenthi blinded the King of Fire with her dancing, this was the working of a spell woven into a perfect performance. With that said, a key aspect of components is that they are always clearly recognizable as being tied to spellcasting. A Phiarlan doesn't hide the fact that they're casting {@spell Enthrall|XPHB} with their song or weaving a {@spell Major Image|XPHB} as they dance; they just make the casting a beautiful thing.

entries.name: Kanon vs Canon
entries: The only change I've suggested to the canon Mark of Shadow is the addition of {@spell Seeming|XPHB} as a possible Spell of the Mark. {@spell Mislead|XPHB} is a fantastic spell for an adventurer or assassin, but Seeming is exceptionally useful for a company of actors. A player character with the Mark of Shadow must choose whether they have access to {@spell Seeming|XPHB} or {@spell Mislead|XPHB}; the feat only grants one of these spells. I do have another house rule tied to House Phiarlan. The {@background House Phiarlan Heir|EFA} background provided in Forge of the Artificer is a good background for an agent of the Serpentine Table, but it doesn't provide proficiency with {@skill Performance} or an instrument, which most Phiarlan heirs should possess. Because of this, I would allow someone who wants to play a typical Phiarlan heir to take the {@background Entertainer|XPHB} background but replace the {@feat Musician|XPHB} feat with the Mark of Shadow.

entries.name: {@filter Focus Items|items|source=|type=|property=shadow (phiarlan/thuranni)}
entries: House Phiarlan has developed a host of tools that channel the powers of the Mark of Shadow. Some of these are designed to enhance an heir's ability to entertain, while others are tools crafted for the Serpentine Table and its agents. Many of these duplicate the effects of existing magic items, though they require the user to possess the Mark of Shadow. The {@item Cloak of Shadows|BakerKanon} is Phiarlan's version of the {@item Cloak of Elvenkind}, while {@item Tialaen Trumps|BakerKanon} are a form of {@item Deck of Illusions}. Two of the House's most important magic items are both variations of the {@item Crystal Ball|XDMG}. The {@item Serpentine Mirror|BakerKanon} is a massive mirror—typically 3 to 4 feet across—which displays images on the surface of the mirror. The Serpentine Table has over a dozen of these Mirrors, including two with the Mind Reading feature. The Mirror of the Mind—a {@item Serpentine Mirror|BakerKanon} with the Telepathy feature—is one of the greatest treasures of the house. It's possible that the Mirror of the Mind was kept in a secret facility in Cyre, in which case it may have been lost in the Mourning; if this is the case, the House is surely desperate to recover it. While the {@item Serpentine Mirror|BakerKanon|Serpentine Mirrors} are tools of espionage, House Phiarlan and House Cannith worked together to develop another variation of {@item Crystal Ball|XDMG}: the {@item Crystal Theater|BakerKanon}. A {@item Crystal Theater|BakerKanon} is a small orb that can scry on a specific location or anchoring focus item; when attached to a Projector Stand, it can cast that image onto a larger surface. This allows audiences to watch theatrical events that are occurring on distant stages. This is a relatively new development, and a Crystal's images are often a little jerky or fuzzy; crystal shows aren't yet threatening the popularity of live performances, but offer an alternative for people who can't afford to attend live shows. {@item Crystal Theater|BakerKanon|Crystal Theaters} and {@item Serpentine Mirror|BakerKanon|Serpentine Mirrors} can only be operated by an heir who has the ability to cast {@spell Clairvoyance} using the Mark of Shadow.

featFluff.name: Mark of Scribing
entries: While this Dragonmark is called the Mark of Scribing, a more accurate term would be communication. {@spell Comprehend Languages|XPHB} is the most basic gift of the Dragonmark, and the one that is typically used during the Test of Siberys. Any bearer of the Mark of Scribing can cast {@spell Comprehend Languages|XPHB} as a {@variantrule Ritual|XPHB}; given time, any word will surrender its meaning to a Sivis heir.

Scribing is a key element of the Dragonmark. Everyone who carries the Mark of Scribing has the ability to inscribe a mystical sigil on any surface. This arcane mark is as unique as a fingerprint. The heir doesn't choose the design of their arcane mark; they touch the surface and draw on the power of the Dragonmark, and their arcane sigil appears. House Sivis keeps a record of the arcane mark of every known heir, so documents can be tracked. After mastering their arcane mark, Sivis heirs learn who to imbue writing with arcane energy. The simplest trick is the explosive rune, an unstable glyph that immediately explodes in a burst of energy. By working more carefully, an heir can weave stable, safe illusions onto a page, creating {@spell Illusory Script|XPHB}. Those with a talent for this work become scroll scribes, crafting permanent {@item Spell Scroll|XDMG|Spell Scrolls}. However, even those who can't produce permanent scrolls can learn to temporarily bind magic to ink. Known as {@spell Santor's Swift Scribing|BakerKanon}, this gift allows a Sivis heir to jot down exactly the spell needed for a difficult situation.

Beyond scribing, the Mark grants a range of powers associated with the spoken word. All those who carry the Mark of Scribing have the ability to cast their words onto the wind, sending a message to any location within ten miles. This is the fundamental principle harnessed by the {@item Speaking Stone|ERLW}. The Stone allows messages to be sent much faster and farther, but it draws on that key power of {@spell Whispering Wind|BakerKanon}. This power played a crucial role in the Last War; while {@item Speaking Stone|ERLW|Speaking Stones} aren't extremely mobile, scouts accompanied by a Sivis heir could use {@spell Whispering Wind|BakerKanon} to report back to their commanders. Heirs with the Lesser Dragonmark gain the gift of {@spell Tongues|XPHB}; not only can they understand any language, they can speak to any creature. {@spell Silence|XPHB} reverses this, allowing a Sivis heir to prevent communication within their sphere of influence. And then there is the remarkable power of {@spell Command|XPHB}. When using this, a Sivis heir draws on their Dragonmark and speaks a single word—a word in a language that no one knows, yet everyone understands. Sivis scholars believe {@spell Command|XPHB} allows the caster to momentarily speak the language of the Progenitors themselves; it resonates on such a primal level that the creature targeted by the word is compelled to obey.

The most dramatic powers of the Mark of Scribing go beyond mere words. {@spell Sending|XPHB} allows the caster to instantly transmit a message over any distance, while {@spell Rary's Telepathic Bond|XPHB|Telepathic Bond} lets an heir forge a mental link between a group of individuals. Both of these gifts bypass language, allowing the recipients to directly understand meaning. Some Sivis heirs are able to reverse this, scrambling a creature's internal communication and blocking their ability to understand their own thoughts; this manifests as Confusion. While {@spell Rary's Telepathic Bond|XPHB|Telepathic Bond} is the power most often associated with the Greater Mark of Scribing, there have been a handful of individuals throughout the history of House Sivis who have instead learned how to communicate with others through the medium of dreams. Because of the rarity of this gift, it has never been incorporated into the services offered by the house, but Tasker's Dream is always searching for heirs with this ability.

entries.name: Kanon vs Canon
entries: In general I prefer to work with existing canon, but it's important to me that the Dragonmarks feel consistent and support the role of the House within the world. House Sivis was built on two spells that don't exist in Fifth Edition. Arcane Mark drove the idea of Sivis notaries, while {@spell Whispering Wind|BakerKanon} was the foundation for the {@item Speaking Stone|ERLW|Speaking Stones}—with the idea that the {@item Speaking Stone|ERLW} carries the message faster and farther, but it's still working off the basic effect of {@spell Whispering Wind|BakerKanon}. Beyond this, the canon Mark of Scribing feat feels significantly more limited than many others. Compare Scribing to Sentinel. Sentinel gets bonuses to {@skill Insight|XPHB} and {@skill Perception|XPHB}, two consistently useful skills; Scribing gets {@skill History|XPHB} and Calligraphy. Sentinel gets {@spell Shield|XPHB}, a spell that's useful in almost every adventure; Scribing gets {@spell Comprehend Languages|XPHB} and {@spell Magic Mouth|XPHB}, again both very situational. So, looking at key changes...

items: I've expanded Gifted Scribe to include Cartography and Forgery, and added Tool Proficiency with one of these. They're all situational, and compared to Making having a bonus with ALL {@item Artisan's Tools|XPHB}, it feels reasonable to me.

I replaced {@spell Message|XPHB} with {@spell Sivis Sigil|BakerKanon}. While I like {@spell Message|XPHB}, I feel that Arcane Mark is an iconic part of Sivis; and I added the Explosive Rune element to give it a little practical punch.

In the base feat, I replaced {@spell Magic Mouth|XPHB} with {@spell Whispering Wind|BakerKanon}, and replaced {@spell Animal Messenger|XPHB} with {@spell Magic Mouth|XPHB} on the Spells of the Mark list. {@spell Animal Messenger|XPHB} is something that makes sense for the Mark of HANDLING. {@spell Whispering Wind|BakerKanon} has specific elements that mirror its expansion by the {@item Speaking Stone|ERLW}: a short message delivered to a particular location. This further means that with the base feat, any Sivis Heir can notarize ({@spell Sivis Sigil|BakerKanon}), translate ({@spell Comprehend Languages|XPHB}), or send a message over a distance ({@spell Whispering Wind|BakerKanon}).

In the Spells of the Mark, I replaced {@spell Arcane Eye|XPHB} with {@spell Santor's Swift Scribing|BakerKanon}. I don't see what {@spell Arcane Eye|XPHB} has to do with words. {@spell Santor's Swift Scribing|BakerKanon|Swift Scribing} gives a Sivis heir a fun, versatile effect that is driven by the act of SCRIBING. It's inspired by {@spell Tasha's Bubbling Cauldron|XPHB} and Rary's Mnemonic Enhancer.

Finally, I added the option of {@spell Whispering Wind|BakerKanon} at 5th level, with the idea that it's the standard gift of the Mark. {@spell Dream|XPHB} is interesting, but assigning it as the sole power of the Greater Mark is problematic on many levels. If Sivis had access to {@spell Dream|XPHB} as a standard ability of the Mark and had it for thousands of years, I'd expect it to play a major role in the House and in history, and we'd need to sort out how Sivis dreamshapers had interacted with the quori. I like it as a rare gift and interesting new development, but {@spell Whispering Wind|BakerKanon} is a simpler spell that fits well with the other gifts of the Mark.

entries.name: {@filter Focus Items|items|source=|type=|property=Scribing (Sivis)}
entries: The {@item Speaking Stone|ERLW} is to House Sivis what the Lightning Rail is to House Orien—a vital tool that drives the core business of the House. But Sivis has developed a number of tools associated with communication. A few examples...

items: A {@item Listening Stone|BakerKanon} appears to be a standard spellshard. However, when activated by a creature with the Mark of Scribing, it records conversations as words on the shard. When activating the shard, the user decides whether it will record all speech that originates within 10 feet of the stone, or just the speech of the creature holding the stone.

A {@item Sivis Slate|BakerKanon} is a sheet of metal. If a creature uses a {@item Lyrriman Quill|BakerKanon} or a {@item Scribe's Pen|ERLW} to write on the Slate and then places the Slate on a sheet of paper or similar material, they can take a {@action Magic|XPHB} action to duplicate the contents of the slate on the page below. They can also use a {@action Magic|XPHB} action to clear the current contents of the Slate.

A {@item Wordbinder|BakerKanon} is a pillar of metal and engraved wood. When a {@item Sivis Slate|BakerKanon} is placed on the Wordbinder, it can be used to transfer the contents to multiple pages simultaneously; this is the basis of Sivis printing.

The Sharn enclave has been experimenting with Echoers. A central Singer works off the premise of {@spell Whispering Wind|BakerKanon}, but rather than directing sound to a single location, the Singer produces multiple gusts that deliver the sound to nearby Echoers, which serve as repeaters transmitting the sound to additional Echoers. In addition to drawing on the Mark of Scribing, the Singer relies on the manifest zone of Sharn to amplify the winds; currently the system is limited to the City of Towers.

spell.name: Kundarak's Ward
entries: You invoke the power of your dragonmark to protect an area of up to 5,000 square feet of floor space. The entire area must be contiguous space within a single structure, but you don't have to ward the entire structure.
When you cast the spell, you may apply any or all of the following effects to the warded area.

items.name: Fog
items.entry: Fog fills all warded corridors (any space with a width of 10 feet or less), making them {@variantrule Heavily Obscured|XPHB}.

items.name: Locks
items.entry: All doors within the warded area are sealed with {@spell Arcane Lock|XPHB}.

items.name: Lost Doors
items.entry: You may choose up to 12 doors in the warded area. These are covered with illusions to make them appear to be unbroken sections of wall.

items.name: Glyph of Warding
items.entry: You may place a single {@spell Glyph of Warding|XPHB} within the warded area. This has the power of a Glyph cast using a 3rd level spell slot, and it is dispelled when Kundarak's Ward expires.

items.name: Privacy
items.entry: Creatures in the area can't be targeted by Divination spells. Sensors created by Divination spells can't appear inside the protected area or pass through the barrier at its perimeter.

items.name: Dimensional Seal
items.entry: Nothing can teleport into or out of the warded area. Planar travel is blocked within the warded area.

entries: When you cast the spell, you can enchant a set of objects-typically coins or rings-that provide protection from its effects. Anyone who is carrying one of these tokens can see through the Fog and Lost Doors effects, open the {@spell Arcane Lock|XPHB|Arcane Locks}, and safely pass the {@spell Glyph of Warding|XPHB}. The tokens have no impact on the Privacy and Dimensional Seal effects, with one restriction; a creature with a token can teleport out of the warded area, but they cannot teleport into it.

{@spell Dispel Magic|XPHB} can be used to target a specific point within an area protected by Kundarak's Ward. If it is successful, it negates all effects of the Ward within a 20 foot radius from that point.

spell.name: Kundarak Seal
entries: You trace a pattern that later unleashes a deadly burst of energy.

entries.name: Sigil of Vengeance
entries: You trace the pattern on your own skin and set the type of damage it will inflict-Acid, Cold, Fire, Lightning, or Thunder. When a creature within 60 feet of you inflicts damage upon you, you may use your {@variantrule Reaction|XPHB} to trigger the sigil. The creature that inflicted damage upon you must make a Dexterity saving throw, taking {@damage 3d8} damage on a failed save or half as much damage on a successful one. The sigil remains in place until it is triggered or until you complete a {@variantrule Long Rest|XPHB}. While you can have multiple sigils prepared, you can only trigger a single sigil with a {@variantrule Reaction|XPHB}.

entries.name: Elemental Trap
entries: You trace the pattern on an object that can be opened or closed (a door, a book, a chest) and set the type of damage it will inflict-Acid, Cold, Fire, Lightning, or Thunder. When someone other than you opens or closes the object the trap detonates; the triggering creature must make a Dexterity saving throw, taking {@damage 3d8} damage on a failed save or half as much damage on a successful one. The warded item is not harmed by this effect. When you cast the spell, you can set it so it will not detonate if the triggering creature either says a password while interacting with the warded object or if they possess an item you designate as a key; you must have this key item in your possession when you cast the spell. The trap remains in place until it detonates.

entries.name: Using A Higher Level Spell Slot
entries: The damage increases by {@scaledice 3d8|1-9|1d8} for each spell slot level above 1.

spell.name: Sivis Sigil
entries: You touch an object, surface, or creature and leave a magical mark. When you cast this spell, choose one of the following effects.

entries.name: Arcane Mark
entries: You place a magical mark on the target. This symbol can be visible or invisible; an invisible mark will be revealed by {@spell Detect Magic|XPHB} or any effect that allows detection of invisible objects. Sivis arcane marks are unique to each caster, but they involve blue and purple lines and often incorporate the {@feat Mark of Scribing|BakerKanon}. If placed on an inanimate object or surface, the mark is permanent. If placed on a living creature, the mark can be removed with {@spell Remove Curse|XPHB}; otherwise it fades in about a month.

entries.name: Explosive Rune
entries: You trace a symbol that dissolves in a deadly surge of power. Make a melee spell attack against a target within reach. On a hit, the target takes {@damage 1d10} damage of a type you choose: Acid, Cold, Fire, Lightning, Poison, Psychic, or Thunder.

entriesHigherLevel.name: Cantrip Upgrade
entries: The damage of Explosive Rune increases by {@dice 1d10} when you reach levels 5 ({@dice 2d10}), 11 ({@dice 3d10}), and 17 ({@dice 4d10}).

spell.name: Whispering Wind
entries: You send a short message or sound to a location that you designate within range. You must be familiar with the target destination, and the whispering wind must have a path to the location; it cannot pass through airtight barriers. The whispering wind is silent, invisible, and intangible until it reaches its destination. It then delivers the message or sound. Note that this targets a location, not an individual; the message is delivered regardless of whether anyone is present to hear it. The whispering wind then dissipates.

The spell can deliver a message of up to 25 words. You may replace five words in the message with a short sound, such as a sharp whistle; if you wish, you could deliver a message of five distinct sounds. You must speak the message or produce the sound you wish to send as part of casting the spell, and the wind reproduces it as spoken. When you cast the spell, you decide on the speed of the wind; it can move as slowly as 1 mile per hour or as quickly as 10 miles per hour.

spell.name: Santor's Swift Scribing

components.m: {@item Lyrriman Quill|BakerKanon}, Parchment, & {@feat Mark of Scribing|BakerKanon}

entries: You scribe a number of {@i Swift Scrolls}, channeling your own arcane energy into the words. You have four charges to distribute when casting this spell. You may expend one charge to create a {@i Swift Scroll} containing a {@filter 1st level spell or cantrip from the Wizard spell list|spells|level=0;1|class=wizard}. You can expend two charges to create a {@i Swift Scroll} holding a {@filter 2nd level spell from the Wizard spell list|spells|level=2|class=wizard}. Any charges you don't use immediately upon casting the spell are lost.
{@i Swift Scrolls} are identical to Spell Scrolls except as noted below.

items: Anyone can read a {@i Swift Scroll} and cast the spell, even if they aren't spellcasters. Casting the spell in this way requires the spell's normal casting time.
When the spell is cast, it uses your saving throw DC and attack bonus.
Spells on {@i Swift Scrolls} can't be copied into spellbooks.
A {@i Swift Scroll} crumbles to dust after 24 hours.

entries: A {@i Swift Scroll} bears the {@feat Mark of Scribing|BakerKanon} and uses shimmering purple-blue ink. Anyone who's had any experience with House Sivis will be aware of the temporary nature of a {@i Swift Scroll}. Once you've cast Santor's Swift Scribing, you can't cast it again until you complete a {@variantrule Long Rest|XPHB}.

spell.name: Ghallanda's Hospitable Hall
entries: You conjure a shimmering door in range that lasts for the duration. The door leads to an extradimensional dwelling and is 5 feet wide and 10 feet tall. This duplicates the effects of {@spell Mordenkainen's Magnificent Mansion|XPHB}, except as noted below:

items: When you cast the spell, you may designate specific creatures that can pass through the door as long as it remains open. Alternatively, you can leave it accessible to the public, in which case any creature can pass through the door while it is open. You can open or close the door (no action required) if you are within 30 feet of it. While closed, the door is imperceptible.
You can create any floor plan you like, but it can't exceed 30 contiguous 10-foot {@variantrule Cube [Area of Effect]|XPHB|Cubes}.
A staff of 15 near-transparent servants attends all who enter, with capabilities as described in {@spell Mordenkainen's Magnificent Mansion|XPHB}.

The Hall can produce sufficient food to serve a three course meal for up to 30 people. It can do this up to three times within a 24 hour period, but at least four hours must pass between each meal. When the Hall prepares food, you must make a Wisdom ({@item Cook's Utensils|XPHB}) check to determine the quality of the food; a result of 10 is palatable, 15 is excellent, and 20 or higher is remarkable. If you're creating a meal you've never tasted before you have {@variantrule Disadvantage|XPHB} on this check; if it's a meal you've made frequently you have {@variantrule Advantage|XPHB} on the check.

spell.name: Mighty Morsel
entries: Ten pellets that provide the sequential flavors of a three course meal appear in your hand and are infused with magic for the duration. A creature can take a {@variantrule Bonus Action|XPHB} to eat one pellet. Eating a pellet restores 1 {@variantrule Hit Points|XPHB|Hit Point}, and the pellet provides enough nourishment to sustain a creature for one day.

You must make a Wisdom ({@item Cook's Utensils|XPHB}) check to determine the quality of the food; a result of 10 is palatable, 15 is excellent, and 20 or higher is remarkable. If you're creating a meal you've never tasted before you have {@variantrule Disadvantage|XPHB} on this check; if it's a meal you've made frequently you have {@variantrule Advantage|XPHB} on the check.
Uneaten pellets disappear when the spell ends.

spell.name: Fortifying Feast
entries: Conjure a small meal for up to five creatures of your choice who remain within range for the spell's entire casting and consume the meal gain the benefits of a {@variantrule Short Rest|XPHB} and also regain {@dice 2d8} {@variantrule Hit Points|XPHB}. A creature can't be affected by this spell again until that creature finishes a {@variantrule Long Rest|XPHB}.

You must make a Wisdom ({@item Cook's Utensils|XPHB}) check to determine the quality of the food; a result of 10 is palatable, 15 is excellent, and 20 or higher is remarkable. If you're creating a meal you've never tasted before you have {@variantrule Disadvantage|XPHB} on this check; if it's a meal you've made frequently you have {@variantrule Advantage|XPHB} on the check.

entriesHigherLevel.name: Using a Higher-Level Spell Slot
entries: The healing increases by {@scaledice 2d8|2-9|1d8} for each spell slot level above 2.

spell.name: Orien Step
entries: You teleport up to 5 feet to an unoccupied space you can see. You cannot teleport through solid objects unless it has an opening (like a portcullis or the bars of a prison cell) or out of manacles and similar restraints unless to escape the {@condition Grappled|XPHB} condition.

item.name: Serpentine Mirror
item.reqAttune: by a Creature with the {@feat Mark of Shadow|BakerKanon} & can cast {@spell Clairvoyance|XPHB}
entries: The Serpentine Mirror is a massive mirror—typically 3 to 4 feet across—which displays images on the surface of the mirror. While touching it, you can cast the {@spell Scrying|XPHB} spell (save {@dc 17}) with it.

The Serpentine Table has over a dozen of these Mirrors, including two with the Mind Reading feature. The Mirror of the Mind—a Serpentine Mirror with the Telepathy feature—is one of the greatest treasures of the house. It's possible that the Mirror of the Mind was kept in a secret facility in Cyre, in which case it may have been lost in the Mourning; if this is the case, the House is surely desperate to recover it.

item.name: Crystal Theater
item.reqAttune: by a Creature with the {@feat Mark of Shadow|BakerKanon} & can cast {@spell Clairvoyance|XPHB}
entries: While touching it, you can cast the {@spell Scrying|XPHB} spell (save {@dc 17}) with it.

A Crystal Theater is a small orb that can scry on a specific location or anchoring focus item; when attached to a Projector Stand, it can cast that image onto a larger surface. This allows audiences to watch theatrical events that are occurring on distant stages. This is a relatively new development, and a Crystal's images are often a little jerky or fuzzy; crystal shows aren't yet threatening the popularity of live performances, but offer an alternative for people who can't afford to attend live shows.

item.name: Tialaen Trumps
entries: {@note Requires the {@feat Mark of Shadow|BakerKanon}}
This box contains a set of cards. A full deck has 34 cards: 32 depicting specific creatures and two with a mirrored surface. A deck found as treasure is usually missing {@dice 1d20 - 1} cards.

The magic of the deck functions only if its cards are drawn at random. You can take a {@action Magic|XPHB} action to draw a card at random from the deck and throw it to the ground at a point within 30 feet of yourself. An illusion of a creature, determined by rolling on the Tialaen Trumps table, forms over the thrown card and remains until dispelled. The illusory creature created by the card looks and behaves like a real creature of its kind, except that it can do no harm. While you are within 120 feet of the illusory creature and can see it, you can take a {@action Magic|XPHB} action to move it anywhere within 30 feet of its card.

Any physical interaction with the illusory creature reveals it to be false, because objects pass through it. A creature that takes a {@action Study|XPHB} action to visually inspect the illusory creature identifies it as an illusion with a successful {@dc 15} Intelligence ({@skill Investigation|XPHB}) check. The illusion lasts until its card is moved or the illusion is dispelled (using a {@spell Dispel Magic|XPHB} spell or a similar effect). When the illusion ends, the image on its card disappears, and that card can't be used again.

item.name: Adamant Chest
entries: {@note Requires the {@feat Mark of Warding|BakerKanon}}

The Adamant Chest is a Tiny model of chest forged from adamantine and set with a sliver of Siberys dragonshard. If someone uses the Adamant Chest as a material component when casting {@spell Leomund's Secret Chest|XPHB|Durbannek's Secret Chest} with the {@feat Mark of Warding|BakerKanon}, they can summon a 3' x 2' x 2' chest from the Ethereal Plane. This chest has the durability of adamantine and is sealed with an {@spell Arcane Lock|XPHB}; the Adamant Chest serves as the key. Casting the spell allows the bearer of the {@feat Mark of Warding|BakerKanon} to summon this chest or to send it back to the Ethereal Plane, but the chest is bound to the Adamant Chest, not to a specific caster; anyone with the {@feat Mark of Warding|BakerKanon} who gains possession of the Adamant Chest can use it to access the associated chest. There is no risk of the spell expiring and the chest being lost in the Ethereal Plane, and an heir can cast the spell with a different material component to summon their own personal chest.

item.name: Keycharm
entries: {@note Requires the {@feat Mark of Warding|BakerKanon}}

A Keycharm is a simple amulet, typically worn around the neck or kept in a pocket. When someone with the {@feat Mark of Warding|BakerKanon} casts {@spell Alarm|XPHB}, {@spell Arcane Lock|XPHB}, {@spell Glyph of Warding|XPHB}, {@spell Kundarak's Ward|BakerKanon} or the Elemental Trap function of {@spell Kundarak Seal|BakerKanon}, they can choose to link the spell to a Keycharm in their possession. If they do this, whoever carries the Keycharm is considered to be the caster of the spell. They are aware of the location of the spell effect and can choose to end the spell or bypass its effects. Most of these spells give the caster an option to set a password or to create objects that serve as keys; the Keycharm takes this a step further, allowing the caster to give full ownership of the spell to the bearer of the Keycharm.

item.name: Locksmith's Ring
item.reqAttune: by a Creature with the {@feat Mark of Warding|BakerKanon}
entries: The Locksmith's Ring is a large iron ring with a single embedded Siberys Dragonshard; it's often used as a keyring, but there is no actual need for it to carry other keys. While attuned to the Locksmith's Ring, a creature with the {@feat Mark of Warding|BakerKanon} can cast {@spell Arcane Lock|XPHB} or {@spell Knock|XPHB} as a {@variantrule Ritual|XPHB}; however, doing so requires 100 GP worth of refined Eberron Dragonshards, which are consumed by the {@variantrule Ritual|XPHB}.

item.name: Calculating Lenses
entries: {@note Requires the {@feat Mark of Scribing|BakerKanon}}

These spectacles allow a Sivis heir to apply the principles of {@spell Comprehend Languages|XPHB} to numbers as well as words. While wearing these glasses, a creature with the {@feat Mark of Scribing|BakerKanon} can take a magic item to perfectly perform basic mathematical operations involving numbers they can see. The {@i Calculating Lenses} can hold a small range of numbers on the surface of the crystal lenses, allowing the wearer to retain the results of calculations until they can be transferred to another surface.

item.name: Lyrriman Quill
entries: {@note Requires the {@feat Mark of Scribing|BakerKanon}}

If you possess the {@feat Mark of Scribing|BakerKanon}, you can use this pen to write on any surface; it draws vivid, purple-blue lines reminiscent of a Dragonmark. You decide whether the writing is visible or invisible, but any creature with the {@feat Mark of Scribing|BakerKanon} can always see the writing. Any creature with the {@feat Mark of Scribing|BakerKanon} can use a magic action to touch invisible writing created with a Lyrriman Quill and render it visible. If you use the quill to write on a living creature, the writing fades after 7 days.
If you use a Lyrriman Quill while creating a {@item Spell Scroll|XDMG}, the time required to create the Scroll is cut in half.

item.name: Listening Stone
entries: {@note Requires the {@feat Mark of Scribing|BakerKanon}}

A {@i Listening Stone} appears to be a standard spellshard. However, when activated by a creature with the {@feat Mark of Scribing|BakerKanon}, it records conversations as words on the shard. When activating the shard, the user decides whether it will record all speech that originates within 10 feet of the stone, or only the speech of the creature holding the stone.

item.name: Sivis Slate
entries: {@note Requires the {@feat Mark of Scribing|BakerKanon}}

A {@i Sivis Slate} is a sheet of metal. If a creature uses a {@item Lyrriman Quill|BakerKanon} or a {@item Scribe's Pen|ERLW} to write on the {@i Slate} and then places the {@i Slate} on a sheet of paper or similar material, they can take a {@action Magic|XPHB} action to duplicate the contents of the {@i Slate} on the page below. They can also use a {@action Magic|XPHB} action to clear the current contents of the {@i Slate}.

item.name: Wordbinder
entries: {@note Requires the {@feat Mark of Scribing|BakerKanon}}

A {@i Wordbinder} is a pillar of metal and engraved wood. When a {@item Sivis Slate|BakerKanon} is placed on the {@i Wordbinder}, it can be used to transfer the contents to multiple pages simultaneously; this is the basis of Sivis printing.

item.name: Bag of Bounty
entries: This leather bag has three charges. A creature with the {@feat Mark of Hospitality|BakerKanon} can take a {@action Magic|XPHB} action to open the bag and cast {@spell Create Food and Water|XPHB}, expending one charge in the process. When using the Bag, a creature can make a Wisdom ({@item Cook's Utensils|XPHB}) check to determine the quality of the food; they don't suffer the {@variantrule Disadvantage|XPHB} that normally applies when casting {@spell Create Food and Water|XPHB} with the {@feat Mark of Hospitality|BakerKanon}. The Bag of Bounty recovers {@dice 1d3} charges at dawn.

item.name: Hosteler's Anchor
item.reqAttune: by a creature with the {@feat Mark of Hospitality|BakerKanon}
entries: The Hosteler's Anchor is a stone brick engraved with the {@feat Mark of Hospitality|BakerKanon}. If the anchor is placed in an extradimensional space created by the creature attuned to Anchor—such as a {@spell Mordenkainen's Magnificent Mansion|XPHB|Magnificent Mansion}, {@spell Ghallanda's Hospitable Hall|BakerKanon|Hospitable Hall}, or {@spell Rope Trick|XPHB}—it can help maintain the space in the absence of the caster. When the duration of the spell expires, the caster can immediately refresh the spell through the Anchor. This requires the creator to expend the appropriate spell slot or charge from a magic item, but it requires no action on the part of the creator. The creator can even set this effect to occur even if the creator is {@condition Unconscious|XPHB}, although they must have the appropriate spell slot or item charge available to be spent. A creature who possesses both a Hosteler's Anchor and a {@item Manor Key|ExploringEberron24} can attune to both items using a single {@variantrule Attunement|XPHB} slot.

item.name: Innkeeper's Gloves
entries: {@note Requires the {@feat Mark of Hospitality|BakerKanon}}
If you possess the {@feat Mark of Hospitality|BakerKanon}, you may use a {@action Magic|XPHB} action to cast {@spell Prestidigitation|XPHB} while wearing these gloves.

item.name: +1 Onatar's Gift
item.reqAttune: by an artificer & a Creature with the {@feat Mark of Making|BakerKanon}
entries: This simple screwdriver can transform into a variety of tools; as a {@action Magic|XPHB} action, you can touch the item and transform it into any type of {@item Artisan's Tools|XPHB|Artisan's Tool} of your choice. Whatever form the tool takes, you are proficient with it.
While holding this tool, you gain a +1 bonus to the spell attack rolls and the saving throw DCs of your Artificer spells.

As a {@action Magic|XPHB} action, you can focus on the tool to channel your creative forces. Choose a cantrip that you don't know from any class list. For 8 hours, you can cast that cantrip, and it counts as an Artificer cantrip for you. Once this property is used, it can't be used again until the next dawn.

item.name: +2 Onatar's Gift
item.reqAttune: by an artificer & a Creature with the {@feat Mark of Making|BakerKanon}
entries: This simple screwdriver can transform into a variety of tools; as a {@action Magic|XPHB} action, you can touch the item and transform it into any type of {@item Artisan's Tools|XPHB|Artisan's Tool} of your choice. Whatever form the tool takes, you are proficient with it.
While holding this tool, you gain a +2 bonus to the spell attack rolls and the saving throw DCs of your Artificer spells.

As a {@action Magic|XPHB} action, you can focus on the tool to channel your creative forces. Choose a cantrip that you don't know from any class list. For 8 hours, you can cast that cantrip, and it counts as an Artificer cantrip for you. Once this property is used, it can't be used again until the next dawn.

item.name: +3 Onatar's Gift
item.reqAttune: by an artificer & a Creature with the {@feat Mark of Making|BakerKanon}
entries: This simple screwdriver can transform into a variety of tools; as a {@action Magic|XPHB} action, you can touch the item and transform it into any type of {@item Artisan's Tools|XPHB|Artisan's Tool} of your choice. Whatever form the tool takes, you are proficient with it.
While holding this tool, you gain a +3 bonus to the spell attack rolls and the saving throw DCs of your Artificer spells.

As a {@action Magic|XPHB} action, you can focus on the tool to channel your creative forces. Choose a cantrip that you don't know from any class list. For 8 hours, you can cast that cantrip, and it counts as an Artificer cantrip for you. Once this property is used, it can't be used again until the next dawn.

item.name: Merrix's Instant Fortress
item.reqAttune: by a creature with the {@feat Mark of Making|BakerKanon}
entries: As a {@action Magic|XPHB} action, you can place this 1-inch adamantine statuette on the ground and, using a command word, cause it to grow rapidly into a square adamantine tower. Repeating the command word causes the tower to revert to statuette form, which works only if the tower is empty. Each creature in the area where the tower appears is pushed to an unoccupied space outside but next to the tower. Objects in the area that aren't being worn or carried are also pushed clear of the tower.

The tower is 20 feet on a side and 30 feet high, with arrow slits on all sides and a battlement atop it. Its interior is divided into two floors, with a ladder, staircase, or ramp (your choice) connecting them. This ladder, staircase, or ramp ends at a trapdoor leading to the roof. When created, the tower has a single door at ground level on the side facing you. The door opens only at your command, which you can issue as a {@variantrule Bonus Action|XPHB}. It is immune to the {@spell Knock|XPHB} spell and similar magic.

{@action Magic|XPHB} prevents the tower from being tipped over. The roof, the door, and the walls each have AC 20; HP 100; {@variantrule Immunity|XPHB} to Bludgeoning, Piercing, and Slashing damage except that which is dealt by siege equipment; and {@variantrule Resistance|XPHB} to all other damage. Shrinking the tower back down to statuette form doesn't repair damage to the tower. Only a {@spell Wish|XPHB} spell can repair the tower (this use of the spell counts as replicating a spell of level 8 or lower). Each casting of Wish causes the tower to regain all its {@variantrule Hit Points|XPHB}.
The Cannith model was created by the same Artificer who developed the {@creature Warforged Titan|ERLW} (the grandfather of the current Merrix d'Cannith).

item.name: Apparatus of Cannith
entries: {@note Requires the {@feat Mark of Making|BakerKanon}}

This item first appears to be a sealed iron barrel weighing 500 pounds. The barrel has a hidden catch, which can be found with a successful {@dc 20} Intelligence ({@skill Investigation|XPHB}) check. Releasing the catch unlocks a hatch at one end of the barrel, allowing two Medium or smaller creatures to crawl inside. Ten levers are set in a row at the far end, each in a neutral position, able to move up or down. When certain levers are used, the apparatus transforms to resemble a giant lobster.

The Apparatus of Cannith is a Large object with the following statistics: {@b AC} 20; {@b HP} 200; {@b Speed} 30 ft., Swim 30 ft. (or 0 ft. for both if the legs aren't extended); {@b Immunity} to Poison and Psychic damage.

To be used as a vehicle, the apparatus requires one pilot. While the apparatus's hatch is closed, the compartment is airtight and watertight. The compartment holds enough air for 10 hours of breathing, divided by the number of breathing creatures inside.
The apparatus floats on water. It can also go underwater to a depth of 900 feet. Below that, the vehicle takes {@damage 2d6} Bludgeoning damage each minute from pressure.

A creature in the compartment can take a {@action Utilize|XPHB} action to move as many as two of the apparatus's levers up or down. After each use, a lever goes back to its neutral position. Each lever, from left to right, functions as shown in the Apparatus of Cannith Levers table.

entries.caption: Apparatus of Cannith Levers
rows: Legs extend, allowing the apparatus to walk and swim.
Legs retract, reducing the apparatus's {@variantrule Speed|XPHB} and {@variantrule Swim Speed|XPHB} to 0 and making it unable to benefit from bonuses to speed.
Side window shutters open (two per side).
Side window shutters close (two per side).
Two claws extend from the front side of the apparatus.
Each extended claw makes the following melee attack: {@hit 8} to hit, reach 5 ft. {@i Hit:} 7 ({@damage 2d6}) Bludgeoning damage.
Each extended claw makes the following melee attack: {@hit 8} to hit, reach 5 ft. {@i Hit:} The target has the {@condition Grappled|XPHB} condition (escape {@dc 15}).
The apparatus walks or swims forward provided its legs are extended.
The apparatus walks or swims backward provided its legs are extended.
The apparatus turns 90 degrees counterclockwise provided its legs are extended.
The apparatus turns 90 degrees clockwise provided its legs are extended.
Eyelike fixtures emit {@variantrule Bright Light|XPHB} in a 30-foot radius and {@variantrule Dim Light|XPHB} for an additional 30 feet.
The apparatus sinks up to 20 feet if it's in liquid.
The apparatus rises up to 20 feet if it's in liquid.
The rear hatch unseals and opens.
The rear hatch closes and seals.

item.name: Talin's Compact Construct, Bronze Griffon
additionalEntries: {@note Duplicated from {@item Figurine of Wondrous Power, Bronze Griffon|XDMG}}
entries: {@note Requires the {@feat Mark of Making|BakerKanon}}

A {@item Talin's Compact Construct|BakerKanon} is an articulated metal model small enough to fit in a pocket. If you take a {@action Magic|XPHB} action to throw the compact construct to a point on the ground within 60 feet of yourself, the compact construct becomes a living creature specified in the compact construct's description below. If the space where the creature would appear is occupied by other creatures or objects, or if there isn't enough space for the creature, the compact construct doesn't become a creature.

The creature is {@variantrule Friendly [Attitude]|XPHB|Friendly} to you and your allies. It understands your languages, obeys your commands, and takes its turn immediately after you on your {@variantrule Initiative|XPHB} count. If you issue no commands, the creature defends itself but takes no other actions.

The creature exists for a duration specific to each compact construct. At the end of the duration, the creature reverts to its compact construct form. It reverts to a compact construct early if its creature form drops to 0 {@variantrule Hit Points|XPHB} or if you take a {@action Magic|XPHB} action while touching the creature to make it revert to compact construct form. When the creature becomes a compact construct again, its property can't be used again until a certain amount of time has passed, as specified below.

entries.name: Bronze Griffon (Uncommon)
entries: This bronze compact construct is of a griffon rampant. It can become a {@creature Griffon|XMM} for up to 6 hours. Once it has been used, it can't be used again until 5 days have passed.

entries.name: Ebony Fly (Uncommon)
entries: This ebony ompact construct, carved in the likeness of a horsefly, can become a {@creature Giant Fly|XDMG} for up to 12 hours and can be ridden as a mount. Once it has been used, it can't be used again until 2 days have passed.

entries.name: Golden Lions (Uncommon)
entries: These gold compact constructs of lions are always created in pairs. You can use one compact construct or both simultaneously. Each can become a {@creature Lion|XMM} for up to 1 hour. Once a lion has been used, it can't be used again until 7 days have passed.

item.name: Talin's Compact Construct, Ivory Goats
additionalEntries: {@note Duplicated from {@item Figurine of Wondrous Power, Ivory Goats|XDMG}}

entries.name: Ivory Goats (Uncommon)
entries: These ivory compact constructs of goats are always created in sets of three. Each goat looks unique and functions differently from the others. Their properties are as follows:

items.name: Goat of Terror
entries: This compact construct can become a {@creature Giant Goat|XMM} for up to 3 hours. The goat can't attack, but you can (harmlessly) remove its horns and use them as weapons. One horn becomes a {@item +1 Lance|XDMG}, and the other becomes a {@item +2 Longsword|XDMG}. Removing a horn requires a {@action Magic|XPHB} action, and the weapons disappear and the horns return when the goat reverts to compact construct form. While you ride the goat, any {@variantrule Hostile [Attitude]|XPHB|Hostile} creature that starts its turn within a 30-foot {@variantrule Emanation [Area of Effect]|XPHB|Emanation} originating from the goat must succeed on a {@dc 15} Wisdom saving throw or have the {@condition Frightened|XPHB} condition for 1 minute, until you are no longer riding the goat, or until the goat reverts to compact construct form. The {@condition Frightened|XPHB} creature repeats the save at the end of each of its turns, ending the effect on itself on a success. Once it succeeds on the save, a creature is immune to this effect for the next 24 hours. Once the compact construct has been used, it can't be used again until 15 days have passed.

items.name: Goat of Traveling
entries: This compact construct can become a Large goat with the same statistics as a {@creature Riding Horse|XMM}. It has 24 charges, and each hour or portion thereof it spends in goat form costs 1 charge. While it has charges, you can use it as often as you wish. When it runs out of charges, it reverts to a compact construct and can't be used again until 7 days have passed, when it regains all expended charges.

items.name: Goat of Travail
entries: This compact construct can become a {@creature Giant Goat|XMM} for up to 3 hours. Once it has been used, it can't be used again until 30 days have passed.

item.name: Talin's Compact Construct, Marble Elephant
additionalEntries: {@note Duplicated from {@item Figurine of Wondrous Power, Marble Elephant|XDMG}}

entries.name: Marble Elephant (Uncommon)
entries: This marble compact construct resembles a trumpeting elephant. It can become an {@creature Elephant|XMM} for up to 24 hours. Once it has been used, it can't be used again until 7 days have passed.

item.name: Talin's Compact Construct, Obsidian Steed
additionalEntries: {@note Duplicated from {@item Figurine of Wondrous Power, Obsidian Steed|XDMG}}
entries: {@note Requires the {@feat Mark of Making|BakerKanon}}
